metadata.document_title: Providing Clinical Evidence of Effectiveness for Human Drug and Biological Products: Guidance for Industry

rules.rule_text: Substantial evidence of effectiveness for a new drug or biological product requires evidence consisting of adequate and well-controlled investigations, including clinical investigations, by experts qualified by scientific training and experience to evaluate the effectiveness of the drug.
rules.source_section: II.A. The Legal Standard
rules.verbatim_quote: Substantial evidence of effectiveness requires evidence consisting of adequate and well-controlled investigations, including clinical investigations, by experts qualified by scientific training and experience to evaluate the effectiveness of the drug.

rules.rule_text: As a general principle, FDA has interpreted the substantial evidence standard to require at least two adequate and well-controlled studies, each convincing on its own, to establish effectiveness. This two-study requirement reflects the need to demonstrate replication of a finding.
rules.source_section: II.A. The Legal Standard
rules.verbatim_quote: As a general principle, FDA has interpreted the substantial evidence standard to require at least two adequate and well-controlled studies, each convincing on its own, to establish effectiveness. This two-study requirement reflects the need to demonstrate replication of a finding.
exceptions: Single study exception under FDA Modernization Act 1997

rules.rule_text: The scientific basis for the two-study requirement is that a single study may produce a false positive result due to: (1) bias in study design or conduct; (2) variability in patient populations; (3) site-specific factors; and (4) the potential for undetected fraud.
rules.source_section: II.B. The Scientific Basis for Replication
rules.verbatim_quote: A single study may produce a false positive result due to: (1) bias in study design or conduct; (2) variability in patient populations; (3) site-specific factors; and (4) the potential for undetected fraud.

rules.rule_text: An adequate and well-controlled clinical investigation must include: a clear statement of study objectives; a design permitting a valid comparison with a control group; a method of selection of subjects that provides adequate assurance that they have the disease/condition studied; adequate measures to minimize bias; and adequate analysis and summary of results.
rules.source_section: II.A. Adequate and Well-Controlled Studies
rules.verbatim_quote: An adequate and well-controlled clinical investigation must include: a clear statement of study objectives; a design permitting a valid comparison with a control group; a method of selection of subjects that provides adequate assurance that they have the disease/condition studied; adequate measures to minimize bias; and adequate analysis and summary of results.

rules.rule_text: The FDA Modernization Act of 1997 (FDAMA) codified the single study exception: where data from one adequate and well-controlled clinical investigation and confirmatory evidence are sufficient to establish effectiveness, FDA may rely upon that evidence.
rules.source_section: II.A. The Legal Standard — FDAMA
rules.verbatim_quote: The FDA Modernization Act of 1997 (FDAMA) codified the single study exception: where data from one adequate and well-controlled clinical investigation and confirmatory evidence are sufficient to establish effectiveness, FDA may rely upon that evidence.

rules.rule_text: Extrapolation of effectiveness from existing studies to a new population or condition may be acceptable when: the drug has demonstrated effectiveness in a related condition; the disease mechanism is similar; and the endpoint used to establish effectiveness is valid for the new population or condition.
rules.source_section: II.C.1. Extrapolation from Existing Studies
rules.verbatim_quote: Extrapolation of effectiveness from existing studies to a new population or condition may be acceptable when: the drug has demonstrated effectiveness in a related condition; the disease mechanism is similar; and the endpoint used to establish effectiveness is valid for the new population or condition.

rules.rule_text: For pediatric indications, effectiveness may be extrapolated from adult studies when the course of the disease and the effects of the drug are sufficiently similar in adults and pediatric patients to permit extrapolation of adult efficacy data to pediatric patients.
rules.source_section: II.C.1. Extrapolation — Pediatric
rules.verbatim_quote: For pediatric indications, effectiveness may be extrapolated from adult studies when the course of the disease and the effects of the drug are sufficiently similar in adults and pediatric patients to permit extrapolation of adult efficacy data to pediatric patients.

rules.rule_text: For modified-release or different-route formulations, effectiveness established for the reference listed drug (RLD) may be extrapolated to the new formulation when bioequivalence to the RLD has been demonstrated and the same active moiety, dose, and indication apply.
rules.source_section: II.C.1. Extrapolation — Modified-Release Formulations
rules.verbatim_quote: For modified-release or different-route formulations, effectiveness established for the reference listed drug may be extrapolated to the new formulation when bioequivalence to the RLD has been demonstrated and the same active moiety, dose, and indication apply.

rules.rule_text: For different dose levels of an approved drug, effectiveness may be established by demonstrating a clinically meaningful pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) relationship between the new dose and the approved dose, without a full replicated clinical trial program.
rules.source_section: II.C.1. Extrapolation — Different Doses
rules.verbatim_quote: For different dose levels of an approved drug, effectiveness may be established by demonstrating a clinically meaningful pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic relationship between the new dose and the approved dose, without a full replicated clinical trial program.

rules.rule_text: A single adequate and well-controlled study plus supporting data from related studies may constitute substantial evidence when the related studies include: studies of different doses or regimens of the same drug; studies of related diseases or conditions; studies using pharmacologic endpoints; or combination therapy studies.
rules.source_section: II.C.2. Single Study Plus Related Study Data
rules.verbatim_quote: A single adequate and well-controlled study plus supporting data from related studies may constitute substantial evidence when the related studies include: studies of different doses or regimens of the same drug; studies of related diseases or conditions; studies using pharmacologic endpoints; or combination therapy studies.

rules.rule_text: Studies of related diseases or conditions may serve as supporting evidence when the diseases share a similar mechanism, pathophysiology, or therapeutic target, and the same endpoints are clinically meaningful for the new condition.
rules.source_section: II.C.2. Single Study Plus Related Study Data
rules.verbatim_quote: Studies of related diseases or conditions may serve as supporting evidence when the diseases share a similar mechanism, pathophysiology, or therapeutic target, and the same endpoints are clinically meaningful for the new condition.

rules.rule_text: Pharmacologic endpoints (e.g., validated surrogate endpoints, biomarkers with established clinical correlation) may serve as supporting evidence in a single study plus confirmatory evidence scenario, provided the surrogate-to-clinical outcome relationship is well established.
rules.source_section: II.C.2. Single Study Plus Related Study Data — Pharmacologic Endpoints
rules.verbatim_quote: Pharmacologic endpoints may serve as supporting evidence in a single study plus confirmatory evidence scenario, provided the surrogate-to-clinical outcome relationship is well established.

rules.rule_text: A single multicenter study may constitute substantial evidence on its own when: the study is large and involves multiple centers; results are internally consistent across centers and subgroups; the primary endpoint is mortality or irreversible morbidity; the p-value is very low (e.g., p<0.001); and/or the drug prevents a disease with 80% or greater efficacy (e.g., vaccines).
rules.source_section: II.C.3. Single Multicenter Study Alone
rules.verbatim_quote: A single multicenter study may constitute substantial evidence on its own when: the study is large and involves multiple centers; results are internally consistent across centers and subgroups; the primary endpoint is mortality or irreversible morbidity; the p-value is very low (e.g., p<0.001); and/or the drug prevents a disease with 80% or greater efficacy.

rules.rule_text: Internal consistency of results across pre-specified subgroups and sites is an essential criterion when relying on a single multicenter trial. A study where significance is driven by a single site or a post-hoc subgroup may not constitute substantial evidence.
rules.source_section: II.C.3. Single Multicenter Study — Internal Consistency
rules.verbatim_quote: Internal consistency of results across pre-specified subgroups and sites is an essential criterion when relying on a single multicenter trial. A study where significance is driven by a single site or a post-hoc subgroup may not constitute substantial evidence.

rules.rule_text: A very low p-value alone (e.g., p<0.001) may support reliance on a single study, but is not sufficient on its own if the study has design limitations, lacks internal consistency, or involves surrogate endpoints without established clinical relevance.
rules.source_section: II.C.3. Single Multicenter Study — p-value Criterion
rules.verbatim_quote: A very low p-value alone may support reliance on a single study, but is not sufficient on its own if the study has design limitations, lacks internal consistency, or involves surrogate endpoints without established clinical relevance.

rules.rule_text: Where replication of a trial result is ethically or practically impossible, a single well-conducted study may constitute substantial evidence, provided the study was large, multicenter, prospectively designed, with pre-specified endpoints and analysis.
rules.source_section: II.C.3. Single Multicenter Study — Ethically Unrepeatable Studies
rules.verbatim_quote: Where replication of a trial result is ethically or practically impossible, a single well-conducted study may constitute substantial evidence, provided the study was large, multicenter, prospectively designed, with pre-specified endpoints and analysis.

rules.rule_text: The vesnarinone example illustrates the risk of relying on a single study: a smaller study showing significant mortality reduction was contradicted by a larger confirmatory trial showing significant mortality increase. This case demonstrates that single-study exceptions carry elevated risk of error.
rules.source_section: II.C.4. Single Study Caveats — Vesnarinone
rules.verbatim_quote: The vesnarinone example illustrates the risk of relying on a single study: a smaller study showing significant mortality reduction was contradicted by a larger confirmatory trial showing significant mortality increase.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors intending to rely on a single study for effectiveness must discuss this approach with FDA early in the development program, before initiation of the pivotal trial, to agree on the study design features that will support the single-study exception.
rules.source_section: II.C.3. Single Multicenter Study — Pre-study Agreement
rules.verbatim_quote: Sponsors intending to rely on a single study for effectiveness must discuss this approach with FDA early in the development program, before initiation of the pivotal trial, to agree on the study design features that will support the single-study exception.

rules.rule_text: For serious or life-threatening diseases with no available therapy, a well-designed single study demonstrating clinically meaningful benefit with an acceptable safety profile may be sufficient for approval under accelerated approval or priority review pathways, with post-approval confirmatory requirements.
rules.source_section: II.C.3. Single Multicenter Study — Serious Unmet Need
rules.verbatim_quote: For serious or life-threatening diseases with no available therapy, a well-designed single study demonstrating clinically meaningful benefit with an acceptable safety profile may be sufficient for approval under accelerated approval or priority review pathways, with post-approval confirmatory requirements.

rules.rule_text: Under the FDAMA single-study exception, confirmatory evidence must be provided in addition to the single adequate and well-controlled study. Confirmatory evidence may include: mechanistic data; PK/PD modeling; studies in related conditions; or data from related populations.
rules.source_section: II.A. FDAMA Single Study Exception — Confirmatory Evidence
rules.verbatim_quote: Under the FDAMA single-study exception, confirmatory evidence must be provided in addition to the single adequate and well-controlled study. Confirmatory evidence may include: mechanistic data; PK/PD modeling; studies in related conditions; or data from related populations.

rules.rule_text: Clinical studies must be conducted in accordance with GCP principles and must have been monitored, audited, and documented according to accepted standards. FDA may perform bioresearch monitoring (BIMO) inspections to verify data integrity.
rules.source_section: III. Documentation Quality — GCP
rules.verbatim_quote: Clinical studies must be conducted in accordance with GCP principles and must have been monitored, audited, and documented according to accepted standards. FDA may perform bioresearch monitoring inspections to verify data integrity.

rules.rule_text: Published literature may support effectiveness claims, but published studies must meet GCP-equivalent standards. The sponsor must provide: the original protocol; the statistical analysis plan; randomization codes; full accounting of all enrolled subjects; individual subject data; and complete safety data.
rules.source_section: III. Documentation Quality — Published Literature
rules.verbatim_quote: Published literature may support effectiveness claims, but published studies must meet GCP-equivalent standards. The sponsor must provide: the original protocol; the statistical analysis plan; randomization codes; full accounting of all enrolled subjects; individual subject data; and complete safety data.

rules.rule_text: Published literature that does not include full subject-level data, a complete protocol, or an audit trail sufficient to verify GCP compliance may not be adequate to serve as a primary source of substantial evidence of effectiveness.
rules.source_section: III. Documentation Quality — Published Literature Limitations
rules.verbatim_quote: Published literature that does not include full subject-level data, a complete protocol, or an audit trail sufficient to verify GCP compliance may not be adequate to serve as a primary source of substantial evidence of effectiveness.

rules.rule_text: An adequate and well-controlled study must have a pre-specified primary endpoint and statistical analysis plan filed before unblinding. Post-hoc analyses or changes to primary endpoints after unblinding do not constitute evidence from an adequate and well-controlled study.
rules.source_section: II.A. Adequate and Well-Controlled Studies — Pre-Specification
rules.verbatim_quote: An adequate and well-controlled study must have a pre-specified primary endpoint and statistical analysis plan filed before unblinding. Post-hoc analyses or changes to primary endpoints after unblinding do not constitute evidence from an adequate and well-controlled study.

rules.rule_text: Control groups in adequate and well-controlled studies may be: placebo concurrent control; dose-comparison concurrent control; active treatment concurrent control; historical control; or no-treatment concurrent control. The choice must be scientifically and ethically justified.
rules.source_section: II.A. Adequate and Well-Controlled Studies — Control Groups
rules.verbatim_quote: Control groups in adequate and well-controlled studies may be: placebo concurrent control; dose-comparison concurrent control; active treatment concurrent control; historical control; or no-treatment concurrent control. The choice must be scientifically and ethically justified.

rules.rule_text: Historical controls are generally not acceptable for demonstrating effectiveness in pivotal trials unless: the disease has a highly predictable outcome without treatment; the effect of the drug is dramatic; or the study involves a patient population in which concurrent controls are ethically infeasible.
rules.source_section: II.A. Historical Controls
rules.verbatim_quote: Historical controls are generally not acceptable for demonstrating effectiveness in pivotal trials unless: the disease has a highly predictable outcome without treatment; the effect of the drug is dramatic; or the study involves a patient population in which concurrent controls are ethically infeasible.
exceptions: Orphan diseases with predictable natural history

rules.rule_text: Randomization must be used in adequate and well-controlled trials to prevent selection bias. The method of randomization must be described in the protocol and the randomization codes retained and available for FDA inspection.
rules.source_section: II.A. Adequate and Well-Controlled Studies — Randomization
rules.verbatim_quote: Randomization must be used in adequate and well-controlled trials to prevent selection bias. The method of randomization must be described in the protocol and the randomization codes retained and available for FDA inspection.

rules.rule_text: Blinding must be implemented where feasible to minimize bias in assessment of outcomes. Double-blind designs are preferred for subjective endpoints. Where blinding is infeasible, the protocol must include documented measures to minimize assessment bias.
rules.source_section: II.A. Adequate and Well-Controlled Studies — Blinding
rules.verbatim_quote: Blinding must be implemented where feasible to minimize bias in assessment of outcomes. Double-blind designs are preferred for subjective endpoints. Where blinding is infeasible, the protocol must include documented measures to minimize assessment bias.

rules.rule_text: All enrolled subjects must be accounted for in the final analysis. Accounting for all subjects includes documentation of dropouts, withdrawals, protocol violations, and lost-to-follow-up subjects, with reasons provided for each.
rules.source_section: III. Documentation Quality — Subject Accounting
rules.verbatim_quote: All enrolled subjects must be accounted for in the final analysis. Accounting for all subjects includes documentation of dropouts, withdrawals, protocol violations, and lost-to-follow-up subjects, with reasons provided for each.

rules.rule_text: For combination products or fixed-dose combination drugs, each component of the combination must be shown to contribute to the effectiveness of the combination. A factorial or component study design is typically required.
rules.source_section: II.C.2. Single Study Plus Related Study Data — Combination Therapy
rules.verbatim_quote: For combination products or fixed-dose combination drugs, each component of the combination must be shown to contribute to the effectiveness of the combination. A factorial or component study design is typically required.

rules.rule_text: Effectiveness claims must be supported by evidence for the specific intended use, route of administration, patient population, and dose. Evidence supporting one indication, dose, or population does not automatically establish effectiveness for a different indication, dose, or population.
rules.source_section: II.C.1. Extrapolation Limitations
rules.verbatim_quote: Effectiveness claims must be supported by evidence for the specific intended use, route of administration, patient population, and dose. Evidence supporting one indication, dose, or population does not automatically establish effectiveness for a different indication, dose, or population.

rules.rule_text: Patient-reported outcome (PRO) instruments used as primary endpoints must be validated for the specific condition and patient population, and must have established content validity, reliability, and sensitivity to change before being used in a pivotal trial.
rules.source_section: II.A. Adequate and Well-Controlled Studies — PRO Endpoints
rules.verbatim_quote: Patient-reported outcome instruments used as primary endpoints must be validated for the specific condition and patient population, and must have established content validity, reliability, and sensitivity to change before being used in a pivotal trial.

rules.rule_text: For drugs intended to treat diseases in special populations (e.g., renal impairment, hepatic impairment, elderly), the pivotal trial must either enroll sufficient numbers of that special population to draw conclusions, or the sponsor must provide evidence that dose adjustments adequately address PK/PD differences.
rules.source_section: II.C.1. Extrapolation — Special Populations
rules.verbatim_quote: For drugs intended to treat diseases in special populations, the pivotal trial must either enroll sufficient numbers of that special population to draw conclusions, or the sponsor must provide evidence that dose adjustments adequately address PK/PD differences.

rules.rule_text: The study population in adequate and well-controlled trials must be representative of the target indication population. Sponsors must provide a rationale if the enrolled population deviates significantly from the intended labeled population.
rules.source_section: II.A. Adequate and Well-Controlled Studies — Study Population
rules.verbatim_quote: The study population in adequate and well-controlled trials must be representative of the target indication population. Sponsors must provide a rationale if the enrolled population deviates significantly from the intended labeled population.

rules.rule_text: The statistical analysis must specify the type I error rate and how multiplicity will be controlled when multiple primary or secondary endpoints are tested. Failure to adequately control type I error may render the study inadequate to serve as evidence of effectiveness.
rules.source_section: II.A. Adequate and Well-Controlled Studies — Statistical Analysis
rules.verbatim_quote: The statistical analysis must specify the type I error rate and how multiplicity will be controlled when multiple primary or secondary endpoints are tested. Failure to adequately control type I error may render the study inadequate to serve as evidence of effectiveness.
